message.content: Adversaries may exploit Kerberos authentication by stealing or forging Kerberos tickets to enable unauthorized access, commonly known as "Pass the Ticket." Kerberos, a widely used authentication protocol in Windows domain environments, involves clients, services, and a Key Distribution Center (KDC). Attackers can abuse this system by extracting Kerberos tickets from the credential cache on Windows, Linux, and macOS systems. On Windows, tools like klist can list and analyze cached tickets. On Linux, Kerberos credentials are stored in the ccache file, managed by the System Security Services Daemon (SSSD) on Redhat-based systems. On macOS, tickets are stored in a ccache format and managed by the KCM daemon. Adversaries can use tools like Kekeo to convert these tickets for cross-platform use. Mitigation strategies include enforcing strong access controls, regularly clearing cached credentials, monitoring for unusual access patterns, and using multi-factor authentication to reduce the risk of ticket theft or forgery.
